speaker: interviewer
text: --1995 I'm conducting an interview with Ruth Kurland, of Chicago, Illinois; tape number three. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, now we're rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, when-- when the-- the lady from the office came, and she saw me in this condition, the water was like ice, and I was-- and I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What condition?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got the cholera. You know what this is? This was a-- a sickness. I can't explain what the cholera is, you know? You run [LAUGHS] to the-- to the-- to-- to wash rooms, in your-- it's a-- it was a epidemic. So they took me to the hospital. And there was, in the room, where I was, there was maybe 10 or 15 women in the same room. And they were all screaming. They want to eat. I didn't want to eat. I was sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I-- my-- they shaved my head. And I was just lying quiet. And I thought, OK. If I'll die. I'll die. What can I do? I didn't-- they didn't think about.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a Uzbek, a doctor, came in and came to my bed. And he gave me a sulfur. I didn't even know what it is. And he said, I give you this. Hide it. Don't show the nurse. And every time, he told me when to take a-- a pill. So I did. Thank God. I came out alive. Everybody was dying in this room. Every woman or girl who was in this room, they were dead. I'm the only one who came out alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they all had cholera too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there-- cholera, that's right. This was the sickness. Yes! We were-- it was epidemic. We were in the same room.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then, after I get better, they wouldn't let you out from the hospital six weeks. You shouldn't-- someone shouldn't catch from you. So, I was going around the hospital doing nothing. You don't have a paper to read-- nothing. What were the conditions like in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't-- at least they give you something, you know, a soup to eat, or something like this. You don't starve like outside. And I was walking around. And there was a very nice Russian nurse. And I said to her, maybe you have something. Give us something to do. I can't-- I'm getting crazy, I can't go around doing nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: She said, what can you do? I said, I know how to sew. She said, can you make little drapes for the-- for the hospital? I said, sure, why not? I did, and she liked it. I made it by hand. And then she said, you know what? When you come out from the hospital, come to my house. I have a daughter. She's your age, and you do something for my daughter-- sew something for her. I said, sure, why not? How old were you at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was already, you know-- you think I remember? 1943. It was before, though. And I came from the hospital. I had to go to work. And my-- my head was, you know, shaved. So I got a little rag-- a little-- rag on my head. And I came to her after work. I had to work for the government. You know, there you cannot not work. I had to go to this factory, and work. And after work, I went to this lady. And I did everything. And then I said, I need a machine where to sew. I cannot by hand. Because I prepare for your daughter a coat and everything. She said you know, we have very nice people here. They are good friend of mine. They have a machine. You can go and-- and do. And I came in there. This was a little, beautiful, clean house, which I didn't seen for a long time. And there was a mother and a daughter. The daughter was an attorney, and the mother was a-- she was such a nice lady. And they used to-- they send them out when the communists came in. They were-- they used to live in Krim. They send them out there. So they have a little house, and they had a cow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is they? Who sent them?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians. The communists send them out. And when-- and I was there, working. And I have what to eat. I worked, and after work, I used to go to the people, and I used to sew. And they gave me food. It was good for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when they-- when I finished for this lady, she's-- the-- the lady from the south-- so she said to me, you know what, Mrs. [NON-ENGLISH], would you do something for us? To sew-- I said sure, why not? And I start doing for them. And then she said, would you move in with us? I-- I start crying. And I said, I can't move in to you, because-- first of all, I wasn't used to so clean, doing in such a beautiful, you know, and I did never.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, don't worry, there's a bed, and there's everything. You're staying with us. And I remember, the first night she put me in a bed with a silk-- silk linen and everything. And I thought, oh my God, I'm in heaven. I didn't expect this. And I stayed there, and then I told--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you sew for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh? Everything-- whatever. Then I thought, in the factory I sewed, for people that I know, better than making shirts. So they-- they recommended I should make dresses for the officers wives. So they-- they made me a dress, and they made me a jacket. I should look a little bit, you know, because these people were coming. And I start doing this-- for them. I made them beautiful dresses. They used to come and said, Riva, make me a dress like in Poland-- for Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: They called you Riva at that point.

speaker: interviewee
text: Riva. And I made them beautiful things they never had in their lives. And I worked for, after work. It was that, from the food, it was the chief that all from the, you know, all the meat went out to me. He was the whole boss over there. And I did for-- for his wife and that thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: One time, he came. He brought me a beautiful silk. In Russia-- silk? Where do you see this? I never saw-- very expensive.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said to me, [LAUGHS] Riva, make me a shirt in two cuts-- a shirt with 100,000 pleats. [LAUGHS] I never did it in my life. But I couldn't say I cannot do it, because-- and this I will never forget. And I-- I was afraid. I was shaking. And they went to work. And I stayed there. And they have a maid who was working for them. And I said--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was at his house?

speaker: interviewee
text: In his house. And I used to go after work. I used to go. And I said-- ashamed-- help me to make this. And I did it. It was beautiful. And he came. Oi, it's wonderful. It's good. So I-- I-- I used to-- it was very good. Then I met my husband there, in-- in Turkestan.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had some friends. And my husband used to live in another-- but then, when he start singing, he had a beautiful voice. He sing so gorgeous. And then we met, and he was going with me. And we stayed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was he doing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was going in a-- in a-- elephant, not-- yeah, riding a-- a elephant and-- and bringing something for, I don't know, to-- to-- to the factories. For a man, it was always easier. You know, he could get better work, and he'd get more paid. But he got torn shirt. I'll never forget. One-- one arm was out, [LAUGHS] and one was in. And-- and then it came. We worked there. And it-- the war is finished-- 1944. The war- war finished, and we stayed there. And then they have a-- a Polish general from England. He made it--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out that the war was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there-- sure, they were celebrating. They were celebrating in-- this was Russia. It was in Asia, but this-- this-- this was Russia. They were celebrating that Hitler is-- he's dead, and that--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in Turkestan you knew what this was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then--

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you had information? You had--

speaker: interviewee
text: --then they told us it was crematorium and that they killed--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- Hitler made crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, who told you? How did you find out about what was going on in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody was talking. Everybody was talking there. Everybody was talking.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, in Turkestan you found out--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what was happening to the Jews in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we happened. Yes. Everybody was talking, because the war was finished, and they were talking. And they were talking on the radio. And they were saying everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware, at any time, about the Jewish calendar? Did you know when the holidays were? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. At one time, when I was by this lady, and-- and it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What lady? The-- the Russian nurse, or the lady with the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian kapo guy. I was-- stayed with them. No, the-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mother and the daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: The nurse was a Russian. She was-- but they were Jewish. And she used to go to-- and she went--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know if they were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were Jewish. They told me. And they went-- she went to a synagogue. And we didn't eat, because it was Yom Kippur. We fast. And she didn't tell us where she is going. She-- she didn't tell her own daughter what synagogue-- where is--

speaker: interviewer
text: The mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: --the other synagogue. Because she said in case there--

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were other Jewish people there? That's why there was a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, because if I'll tell you, you know. And in case they catch me, it's not good for you. So she didn't tell your own daughter. But she was-- they were very good to me. They were so good to me. When I went in the morning to work, she-- she made me gloves. She made me a hat. She-- she walked-- watched me, to go out, to look good. I'll never forget these people. And, I remember, there was a-- one time you have-- there was a election. And something-- the daughter-- you know, there is election-- everybody for Stalin. You have to put for Stalin. She made a mistake, and she put something else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the people came running to her and said, listen. You made a mistake. Come and check.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because if she wouldn't check what it is. And then they would arrest you. In-- in Russia, for-- for-- you did something, you was-- you go to jail. They don't bother me too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she went. And she-- and then, when it came out the-- the Polish general something said, you should let out the Polish people back to Poland. Now, Poland was-- became Communist. But they-- we didn't know what was, or when was.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you had to registered-- and all the Polish people who want to go. You know, we want to go home, or something. I don't know where. And my husband already made it. I-- he should go with me in the same wagon. This was like cattle wagon. So this-- the lady from the house, told him, don't let her run out from the train, because we had to bring water down. And he was watching me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Weeks. It took a long time. And every time the train stopped, my husband used to-- to sing. And all the people used to come and listen to him, because he-- he sings just beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you survive on the train? What did you have to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we-- we got food. They gave us food to take with us. And we made a-- like--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you made the trip on that convoy?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of people-- a long, long, long train. All the Polish-- what it was-- Polish citizens came out. And we came to Krakow, Poland. And in Krakow, Poland, whoever was like a husband and wife, they gave them where to live. And for us, we went in a Kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know what a Kibbutz is? You live all together, like in Israel-- like kibbutz. And this was important. And we stayed there. In Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Krakow. And there was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you find when you get there-- when you got there? What-- what was--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Krakow?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see? Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see nothing here, because they were telling this. When I said-- but, only thing, the people in Krakow, the Polish people, used to say, look at-- they're still alive. We thought all the Jews are-- are dead. And see, they're coming. They took our coal, and they-- Russia, and they send us Jews. And we were there. We stayed in this. And then one time they used to come, the Polish--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there in the Kibbutz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the women used to go, like servant, or-- in-- in homes. And they used to get paid. I find a dressmaker. And I worked. And my husband used to give concerts. And then, he used to sit by the door-- this was a Universitet-- with a gun, and watch who was coming in, because there was a lot of Polish people who used to come and kill Jews. There was, in Kielce-- I think in Kielce it was it-- that they came in a kibbutz, and they killed, while-- I think were two of the young people in the kibbutz. So they--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you experienced a lot of antisemitism--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --when you came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: This of-- yes. So we always have to be on guard. There was always, at night, always boys to go around with a-- with a rifle. And there was 500 people there. I think there was maybe 40 girls. The rest was boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the living conditions like?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we worked, but they didn't give us-- you know, they didn't give us what we-- they gave us a-- a bread. It was, take a hike and heck. It so, so hard, You know, some others stole the money. I don't know whatever they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who ran this Kibbutz? Who organized this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was organized from Israel. But the people who were there, they-- they wasn't very-- very honest. And at night, they told us to be-- yes, the-- the police were watching us. You know, and the police wasn't-- didn't kill though. The only thing, people-- that we were afraid, because we find over there they killed in the Kibbutz, but two of the four of the people. So they told us every-- every girl, where she stays. We had like-- they made with us, every time, we should be ready. These girls stays with these boys, these girls stay with these boys. And they told us, in case something happened, everybody should be quiet. Now, a boy who-- who had the-- this rifle and accidentally shoot out, the rifle shoot out at him. So we thought that the-- they coming to kill us!

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did the girls do? They started screaming. [LAUGHS] We were supposed to be-- told to be quiet, but instead quiet, they screamed. Well, thank God it was OK. How far was Krakow from Rovno?

speaker: interviewee
text: Far away, yes. Rovno was near the-- the Russian border. Krakow is near the, I think, the German border-- far away there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at this point you knew nothing about your family, what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I find that nobody is alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you find that out at that-- that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: They told us that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is they?

speaker: interviewee
text: --they took-- people who came. They took, in Rovno, the 60,000 Jewish people, and there they took them to the woods, and they made-- they dig holes. And they were ready to kill all of them-- women, men, children, my parents, everybody. And the Rabbi, Rabbi [NON-ENGLISH], he talked to the people. And he told them, this is from God. And nothing we can do about it. That's made from the-- so the-- the-- it-- the Germans thought that he's a friend of them. So they came to him and said, all right, we'll let you live. He said no. I don't want to live. If my city is going, I'm coming with the city. And he got killed with my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you had an eyewitness that told you that?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's always a eyewitness. Whatever it was, somebody comes out alive, and told us. And then, I-- we got married in Krakow. We there stayed there, maybe three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the Kibbutz you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And my wedding was 500 young people, and we had herring and bread, with tea, But we went to a Rabbi, and we got married. And then, they decided we had to go out from Poland, to go to-- to Austria or Germany. From there maybe we'll go to Israel. And there was people who was, you know, was helping us. I don't know if they came from Israel or from where. We were 29 people, and we were supposed to go on a train to take us there. And it was winter. I already had a cold, because I was working. And my husband didn't look too much Jewish. And another friend, he was exactly like a-- like a-- not Jew. So we went. And I-- they told us, don't sit together, sit three and three. I had a girlfriend, she was very beautiful, and she didn't speak good Polish. When she start to use-- talking Polish, you see right away that she's Jewish. By--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were going to Germany at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, were were going-- where to go to the-- to the borders. We had to go to Czechoslovakia, all. And [SIGHS] I don't know. I was sitting there, and I see a commander from the train. He was dressed nice. And two people came in-- torn jackets, and this, and they have eggs.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he gave them, to drink, vodka, or I don't know-- whatever. And I thought to myself, what is this man doing with these two people-- why they drink so much?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was a very good-- I used to listen very good. I can't still good, but I listen still. And I was sitting. I-- you know, I didn't know. And I heard that he said to them, well, you go there. And tell them where, and he have everything. You have swords. And you have water. And if you kill them, you can wash up everything. And, right away, they noticed 29 people are going-- Jewish people. And the first thing, we has to go through-- through a tunnel, they said. And-- by the tunnel, you'll take this man-- my girlfriend's husband-- she'll take him for a sample and see what-- how they work out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I called right away my-- my girlfriend's husband, and I said, listen. They-- I just listened. They want to kill us, but first they want to take you. When you see we coming for a tunnel, go hide someplace. Don't sit in the same place where you were sitting. So he did. And these-- the-- these men were going around and asking, where is this man? Where is the Jew? We can't find him. Where is he? We can't find him.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, we get through the tunnel, and he's alive. And then, she said let's all sit together, they know, already, that we are, together 29 people and they are Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, I said to this-- to who was sitting with us-- I says, you know what? When the train stops go out, and go to a policeman, and ask him what time it is. Don't tell them that they want to kill us. Well, maybe he's a killer too. You couldn't trust anybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he went down, and he went to a policeman asking what time it is. This man ran out from the train, they were running away. And we came to-- thank God we made it. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you arrive?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived near-- I don't remember. But I know we were in a-- in a headache there. They hold us. And then we supposed to go through the Russian soldiers. They told us not to speak Jewish, not to speak Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is they? Who told you that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people who took us-- the-- this was the-- I think the Jewish people who took us there. He told us, don't-- don't say anything. The best thing is to be quiet, because we have to go through the Russia. And they-- they wouldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, we were there. And they came up on the train, and they looked us over. And they let us go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so from there, we came to Czechoslovakia. But when we came out from Poland, and we had to go there on hills, the Polish people, whatever anybody have something, they took away. They got a ring, or you got anything-- we didn't have much. They cleaned us out-- everything. But in Czechoslovakia, the people are so nice. I still remember. They were very nice to us. And we stayed there. They gave us food. They took sick people to the hospital. And from there, we had to go to-- [SIGHS] to-- they took us to Austria. In Austria, to-- we came to Salzburg. And in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did-- why did they take you to Salzburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the DP camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: The DP camp, I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Salzburg gave out DP camps. And they took us to the DP camps, which the American took care of us. And, we stayed there for a while. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What is a DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: A DP camp is just like a-- they have little places were to sleep, and they give you something to eat. And-- and there was a director. She was from the United-- from from America. But she she steal all the-- the food what they were supposed to give us. And they didn't give us much what to eat. So we always ran out of, you know-- there was nothing. You-- you-- they give you a little bit of water or something to-- it wasn't-- it wasn't good.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: So they find out-- nothing. There was nothing to do. So, my husband find out that in Hallein is a new DP, and they need policemen. So a lot of the boys, they went to Hallein, and they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is Hallein?

speaker: interviewee
text: By Salzburg. This what I have the-- and-- and then, I came there. We stayed there. My daughter was born there in 1947. My husband, he was very intelligent, and a very nice man. Because there was 5,000 people in-- there wasn't to-- too big places to live. So one used to fight with the others. You know all kind of people. And they made my husband for chief of police, because he was very-- he was very nice. He used to come in and make everybody to be friends, and to-- not to fight, and that. And then, he used to sing for the-- for this camp. He used to sing. There was a radio. And he used to sing. And he used to go to give concerts. In Germany, he went to [NON-ENGLISH] and give concerts.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's how he earned his living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No, in Salzburg-- I-- I skipped this. We were in Salzburg. A cantor came that he was looking for talents. They was making the high holidays for the American soldiers. And they-- everybody told him that my husband is a good singer. And he took him in. And he was making the high holidays there. But in Hallein-- we stayed in Hallein. And we got what to eat. And then I was riding--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of accommodations did you have there?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Well, the room was like this. It was like two couples. But when the-- when my husband-- we got a position better, we got a room for ourselves; and a little kitchen by the side. And we stayed, and we want to-- to live. You know--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did people do at that point to try and find their relatives, or find out what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I write to Israel to find if my brother is alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you write to?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a-- a-- somebody gave me a phone number in Jerusalem-- or Jerusalem something. And I wrote there. And I received a letter that my brother is alive, and he's in Israel with a wife and a child. And I wrote.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find that out?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1947 I think-- 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there some kind of a central place where people went to-- to give their names and family members--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, always, always.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to see, to find out--

speaker: interviewee
text: If you were looking for somebody, if you have relatives, if you have sometime friends, If you--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in that DP camp, in Hallein?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were maybe 5,000-- 5,000 people. And there we were waiting.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were all displaced Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They're all Jewish people. There wasn't a Christian. But my doctor was a-- was-- was Ukrainian. And he saved my life and my--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Hallein?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hallein. My daughter was born in Hallein. And [SIGHS] when I got-- from everything, I got high blood pressure. And before, the Jewish doctors want to take away the baby from you. They said you can't have it, because if you'll have a baby you'll die.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, if I lost my whole family, and I can't have a child, I don't want to live either. So this Ukraine doctor came, and I went to him. And he said, don't worry, I'll see that you'll have a-- a-- a-- a child. And don't listen to anybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when my daughter was born, he-- he-- he helped me. He was just wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Hallein?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think a long time-- we-- '45 to 1946. We were five years in-- in Hallein. And then, we got papers to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you meet to a lot of survivors from camps, from Germany? Did you find out more--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a lot of people--

speaker: interviewer
text: --in that camp, about what went on in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: --from Poland, from-- as a matter of fact, before I worked with children from the Holocaust, I went in a seminar. Before my daughter was born, I went to Linz, in a seminar. And when I came back, I worked with children. Usually, the children were from Russia. People came from Russia, their children. Because the people who came from the-- from the camp from Germany didn't have children. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were able to find out more about what took place in Germany during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. They were telling all the-- the terrible things-- the way they burned the people, the way they-- it was-- you couldn't-- it's unbelievable. It's unbelievable the way that-- there was one man, with his wife, by us, and he couldn't eat meat, because when he was in-- in the concentration camp, they-- he was-- his work was to take the people and put it in the oven to burn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, he couldn't help it, you know? They put him there. And he put the people. So he never could eat meat. And he get-- and he couldn't stand it, because it was so terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] We stayed there till 1946. We went to Canada. Cousins took us to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how did you find out you had cousins in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- [SIGHS] When we were in Hallein, my husband find his brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, it was your brother's family-- your husband's family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband's family. Everybody was killed. Only he-- he survived. There was a little girl, nine years old, and she-- she lived till-- till they liberate. And then she drank the water that they poisoned-- [?Hitler?] poisoned the water. She was the only-- first one who took a little bit of water, and she-- she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my husband remembered the name from his uncle in the United States. And he wrote a letter to this uncle. And he find out that his brother is alive.
